coredata.dc:title: Greener production of a starch-based nanohybrid material (core-shell) for the simultaneous extraction of persistent organic pollutants in shrimp samples
coredata.dc:creator: de Jesus, Jemmyson Romário
Alfredo, Lucas Hestevan Malta
de Carvalho, Jéssica Passos
de Jesus, Camilo Bruno Ramos
Moreira Novaes, Fábio Junior
de Queiroz, Maria Eliana Lopes Ribeiro
Garcia, Paulo de Tarso
coredata.dc:description: Here, a novel nanohybrid material (Ag@CD@ANS) based on oat starch was produced, characterized, and applied to extract persistent organic pollutants in a shrimp sample. By the characterization experiments, Ag@CD@ANS was successfully synthesized. The functionalization of the material by 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulphonic acid (ANS) was confirmed using the infrared technique and CHN elemental analysis. The isotherm study showed that the material has a high adsorption capacity for the pesticides of interest (flutriafol, atrazine, heptachlor, DDT and bifenthrin) allowing their extraction from shrimp samples. The optimal condition for extraction was obtained using multivariate analysis. The nature of the elution solvent (hexane, methanol, acetonitrile) and the mass ratio between sample:adsorbent (1:1; 1:5 and 1:10) were the evaluated factors for extraction using Ag@CD@ANS and commercial adsorbents (neutral alumina, octadecyl, silica gel). From the multivariate analysis, it was observed that the optimal condition for pesticide extraction using Ag@CD@ANS was reached, using a 1:5 ratio (sample:adsorbent) and acetonitrile (10 mL) as elution solvent. For the commercial adsorbents, the optimal condition for pesticide extraction was reached, using a 1:3 ratio (sample:adsorbent), acetonitrile (10 mL) and neutral alumina as commercial adsorbent. Ag@CD@ANS efficiency was compared with an optimal commercial adsorbent (neutral alumina). No significant difference (p < 0.05) between neutral alumina and Ag@CD@ANS was observed. Recoveries ranging from 75 to 105 % with coefficients of variation ≤ 15 % (n = 3) were obtained using neutral alumina while using Ag@CD@ANS, recoveries ranging from 73 to 102 %, with coefficient of variation ≤ 13 % (n = 3) were obtained for the target pesticides. Limits of detection ranging from 0.5 to 1.0 µg Kg−1 and limits of quantification ranging from 1.6 to 3.3 µg Kg−1 were reached. The results demonstrated that Ag@CD@ANS can alternatively be used as a support for the extraction of persistent organic pollutants, having the advantage of being reusable for up to three cycles.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a novel nanohybrid material (Ag@CD@ANS) based on oat starch was produced, characterized, and applied to extract persistent organic pollutants in a shrimp sample. By the characterization experiments, Ag@CD@ANS was successfully synthesized. The functionalization of the material by 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulphonic acid (ANS) was confirmed using the infrared technique and CHN elemental analysis. The isotherm study showed that the material has a high adsorption capacity for the pesticides of interest (flutriafol, atrazine, heptachlor, DDT and bifenthrin) allowing their extraction from shrimp samples. The optimal condition for extraction was obtained using multivariate analysis. The nature of the elution solvent (hexane, methanol, acetonitrile) and the mass ratio between sample:adsorbent (1:1; 1:5 and 1:10) were the evaluated factors for extraction using Ag@CD@ANS and commercial adsorbents (neutral alumina, octadecyl, silica gel). From the multivariate analysis, it was observed that the optimal condition for pesticide extraction using Ag@CD@ANS was reached, using a 1:5 ratio (sample:adsorbent) and acetonitrile (10 mL) as elution solvent. For the commercial adsorbents, the optimal condition for pesticide extraction was reached, using a 1:3 ratio (sample:adsorbent), acetonitrile (10 mL) and neutral alumina as commercial adsorbent. Ag@CD@ANS efficiency was compared with an optimal commercial adsorbent (neutral alumina). No significant difference (p < 0.05) between neutral alumina and Ag@CD@ANS was observed. Recoveries ranging from 75 to 105 % with coefficients of variation ≤ 15 % (n = 3) were obtained using neutral alumina while using Ag@CD@ANS, recoveries ranging from 73 to 102 %, with coefficient of variation ≤ 13 % (n = 3) were obtained for the target pesticides. Limits of detection ranging from 0.5 to 1.0 µg Kg−1 and limits of quantification ranging from 1.6 to 3.3 µg Kg−1 were reached. The results demonstrated that Ag@CD@ANS can alternatively be used as a support for the extraction of persistent organic pollutants, having the advantage of being reusable for up to three cycles.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The existence of food contaminants, such as persistent organic pollutants (POPs), is an important problem of food safety [1]. POPs are chemicals of global concern due to their potential for long-distance transport, persistence in the environment, ability to biomagnify and bioaccumulate in ecosystems, as well as their significant negative effects on human health and the environment [2]. The most founded POPs are organochlorine and organophosphate pesticides, as well as unintentional by-products of many industrial processes, especially polychlorinated dibenzofurans (PCDF) and polychlorinated dibenzo-p-dioxins (PCDD), commonly known as dioxins [2]. These organic contaminants can enter the aquatic environment mainly through rainfall, which carries these pollutants through leaching. Contamination of aquatic environments by pesticides ends up directly contaminating marine life and humans indirectly through the food chain [3]. Long-term exposure to these pollutants in humans and the aquatic environment has raised major health concerns due to the carcinogenic, immunocompromised, endocrine-disrupting, and dysfunctional effects associated with these compounds [2,3].

One of the most consumed foods in the world is shrimp, a crustacean of the order Decapoda (ten feet, in Greek) that can be of marine or freshwater origin [4]. This crustacean has great economic and nutritional importance and is therefore widely marketed and consumed worldwide. In this sense, aquaculture, which involves the farming of fish, crustaceans, mollusks, and other organisms usually in a controlled system, is one of the fastest-growing sectors in food production globally [5]. However, there have been increasing reports of chemicals and other substances on aquaculture farms, as well as food contamination in edible aquatic foods, including those sold in public markets [6]. Given this reality, it is essential to invest in reliable, sensitive, and selective analytical methods to monitor and control the contamination of food by agricultural and industrial residues.

Due to the high complexity of food matrices and because contaminants are usually present at trace levels, a sample treatment step is required [7,8]. However, the sample preparation step represents one of the bottlenecks in analytical methods for detecting contamination in food matrices, because it is necessary not only to isolate and enrich the analytes of interest, but also to remove interfering substances, making it a difficult task [1,9]. Several sample preparation procedures have been widely used in recent decades to control and monitor POPs in food matrices, such as QuEChERS [10], salt-assisted liquid-liquid extraction (SALLE) [11], ionic-liquid extraction (ILE) [12] and solid phase extraction (SPE) and its variations (matrix solid phase dispersion, MSPD, solid phase microextraction, SPME and dispersive solid phase extraction, D-SPE) [8,12,13]. Specifically, MSPD is a suitable extraction technique that provides high analyte enrichment, extract cleanup, and preconcentration in just one step [7]. Furthermore, MSPD is an environmentally friendly technique, compatible with various types of adsorbents, suitable for most biological, environmental and food samples, requiring very low volumes of sample and solvent (mL) and the small amount of adsorbent material (mg) required for (micro)column packaging [14].

In MSPD, the type of sorbent and its interaction with the analyte play an important role in the efficiency of analyte extraction and preconcentration [15]. Adsorbent materials commonly used in MSPD cartridges include octadecyl, silica gel, alumina, among others, however many of these adsorbents are not reusable, being a potential emerging pollutant. In this regard, research into the synthesis and performance of various new eco-friendly materials has become a research hotspot in recent years. For example, carbonaceous nanomaterials have also been employed and integrated with SPE techniques to extract organic pollutants, providing additional retention sites than previous adsorbents [16]. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as new nanomaterials with increasing applications in different areas [9,17]. For instance, Carneiro et al. [18] described a fluorescent sensing strategy based on CD for the identification of pesticides (propanyl, parathion, dimethoate, chlorpyrifos, and pyrimicarb) in food (carrot, orange, and pepper). As result, it was found that in pepper extract the sensitivity of the detection strategy was 250 ng mL−1 [18]. In another study, Li et al. [19] showed that CD can be applied as a bioplatform for colorimetric and fluorimetric detection of organophosphate pesticides in complex samples [19]. The dual assay provided good sensitivity for rapid detection of organophosphate pesticides with a detection limit of 0.4 ng mL−1. From these studies, it is possible to observe that CDs can be a powerful tool to identify pesticides in food. Obviously, these successful applications using CD can be attributed to its advantages, such as being an inexpensive material, easily scalable, high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, excellent chemical stability, and inertness, easy preparation and functionalization [20]. Furthermore, they can be prepared using a variety of methods, including green synthesis procedures that employ readily available and inexpensive resources as a starting material, without the use of chemicals for preparation [21]. Despite the excellent characteristics of CDs, they have not yet been fully explored in analytical applications that employ the extraction of organic contaminants in shrimp samples as an adsorbent.

Core-shell nanoparticles (NPs) have attracted attention in analytical research due to their versatility, allowing modifications to suit various applications, changing the nature or composition of the core and shell material. Furthermore, core-shell NPs offer distinct advantages over single NPs, including improved biocompatibility, reduced cytotoxicity, and reuse potential [22,23].

In this study, we demonstrate the synthesis and application of a new core-shell nanohybrid material based on silver nanoparticles (AgNP, core) and CDs (shell). The use of AgNP as the nucleus (core) in core-shell materials aims to increase the mechanical stability of the material prepared for applications in adsorption processes that use mechanical milling methods, such as the MSPD technique [24]. Furthermore, due to their electronic and chemical properties, AgNP has a high surface area and a high affinity for many organic and inorganic compounds, allowing them to be used as a nucleus in core-shell materials [25]. These characteristics result in a material with high chemical, thermal and mechanical stability with high surface area available for the adsorption of target molecules, thus increasing the adsorption capacity of the material [25]. Furthermore, AgNP can be regenerated or recovered after the adsorption process [26]. This is particularly valuable in pollutant removal applications where regeneration of the adsorbent material is desirable for continuous and sustainable use.

In turn, CDs can be used to coat the core of materials, providing protection against degradation [25]. Furthermore, CDs can be used to modify the surface of the core, giving it new features, such as greater stability in adverse conditions [27]. In addition, the surface of carbon dots can be easily functionalized, allowing the binding of specific groups or molecules of interest, and the creation of hybrid materials with combined properties, including high adsorption capacity [28].

In this scenario, oat starch was used as a carbon precursor (CDs) for functionalization using 1,2-naphthoquinone-4-sulphonic acid (ANS). Ag@CD@ANS was tested as an alternative MSPD adsorbent for pesticide extraction (atrazine, heptachlor, DDT, flutriafol, and bifenthrin) in shrimp samples, resulting in a simple, economical and environmentally sustainable product. The justification for choosing these pesticides is based on the representation of the main classes of pesticides used in agricultural production. For instance, flutriafol (which belongs to the triazole class) is used in agriculture to control fungal diseases in crops, inhibiting the growth of fungi [29]. Atrazine (which belongs to the triazine class) is used to control the growth of weeds in agricultural crops [30]. Heptachlor is a pesticide belonging to the chlorophenols class and widely used as an insecticide in agricultural production [31]. DDT is an organochlorine pesticide that, despite being banned in some countries, continues to be used to control insect pests in agriculture [32]. Bifenthrin is a synthetic pyrethroid insecticide widely used in agriculture and residential pest control to target a variety of insect pests [33].

All chemicals above mentioned can affect marine life mainly through leaching processes, surface runoff, industrial and agricultural effluents. Ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled to a diode array detector (UPLC-DAD) was used to evaluate the quantitative and reproducible aspects of the method. It is important to highlight that, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first study reporting the preparation of a nanohybrid material based in Ag NPs and CD functionalization (Ag@CD@ANS) for use as an adsorbent in the extraction of pesticides from a shrimp sample using the MSPD technique.

LC grade solvents (methanol, hexane and acetonitrile) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Certified standards atrazine, heptachlor, DDT, flutriafol and bifenthrin were obtained from Fluka (Steinheim, Germany). All standards employed were at least of 98 % purity. Ultrapure water was obtained by purifying of distilled water using a Milli-Q gradient system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). Silica gel (70–270 mesh), octadecyl (C18, 50 μm) was obtained from Agilent Technologies (USA) and neutral alumina (70–290 mesh) was obtained from Vetec (Rio de Janeiro, Brazil). Silver nitrate (AgNO3) and trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Chemicals were used as received without further purification.

To carry out the extractions, mixed solutions were prepared from standard stock solutions (1 mg L−1) of the five pesticides. Physical-chemical parameters and classes of the pesticides can be seen in Table S1. Fig. S1 shows the molecular structures of the compounds.

Samples (0.500 Kg) of chemical-free shrimp (Macrobrachium acanthurus) were obtained from shrimp farms in the state of Minas Gerais (MG), Brazil and subsequently retailed at a street market in the city of Viçosa/MG. The collected samples were promptly placed on ice and transported to the laboratory. Next, the shrimp were subjected to a deionized water rinse, and subsequently homogenized for 3 min, using a food processor. The homogenized material was then transferred to a polyethylene bag, hermetically sealed, and stored at a temperature of −20 °C for 24 h. Then, the homogenized material was lyophilized for a period of 72 h and subsequently kept in a desiccator under room temperature conditions.

For the synthesis of the nanohybrid material, the hydrothermal method of [34] was used with few modifications. Initially, 3.00 g of oats were placed in a 50.00 mL container and 25.00 mL of ultrapure water was added. After 15 min, with periodic stirring, 15.00 mL of the suspended starch was transferred to a 20.00 mL teflon and 0.25 g of AgNO3 + 0.30 g Na3C6H5O7 was dissolved in the mixture. Then, the solution was transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave reactor with PTFE chamber. The reactor was heated in an electric oven at 230 ⁰C and maximum pressure of 3 Mpa for approximately 8 h. In the end, after the reactor had cooled to room temperature, the solution was transferred to a Falcon tube (15 mL) and centrifuged. To the formed precipitate, 8.0 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH and 1.0 g of ANS were added (Fig. 1 ). The material was stirred for 2 h and then centrifuged (8000 rpm) for 40 min. The supernatant was removed, and the material dried in an oven for 6 h at 60 °C. Next, the material was stored at room temperature for further characterization and application.

The functionalization of the nanomaterial (Ag@CD) was verified by infrared spectroscopy in a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific inc., Waltham, MA, USA) equipped with an ATR accessory with a germanium crystal. Monitoring was done in the mid-infrared range (4000–400 cm−1). Crystal structures were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) of powder obtained at room temperature using Rigaku diffractometer with Bragg-Brentano geometry in continuous mode in the 2θ range from 25 to 700 using CuKα radiation. The morphology of the nanomaterial was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy-SEM (JSM6510LV) with voltage acceleration ranging from 0.3 kV to 30 kV and transmission electron microscope-TEM (JEOL JEM-2100F) operated at 200 kV. Thermal stability was evaluated by Thermogravimetry (TGA) in a thermobalance (2950, TA Instruments) heated from 25 to 800 °C, with a heating of 10 °C min−1 under nitrogen flow (50 mL min−1). The elemental analysis of carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen (CHN) present in the synthesized material was carried out in a Vario MACRO instrument, equipped with a conductivity detector. The combustion tube was assembled at 1150 °C and the reduction tube at 850 °C. Sulfanilamide was used as CHN standard (C = 41.81 %, N = 16.26 %, H = 4.65 %, S = 18.62 %;% by weight). Particle size was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS), before the measurements, the samples were diluted in ultrapure water and were subjected to ultrasound for 5 min. The measurement of zeta potential was conducted through electrophoretic mobility using a Zetasizer® nano-ZS ZEN 3600 (Malvern, UK). Prior to the measurements, the samples were diluted in a PBS buffer solution (0.10 mmol L−1, pH 7.5 ± 0.3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the point of zero charge (pHPZC)
ce:section.ce:para:
The determination of the point of zero charge (pHPZC) of the nanomaterial, an important factor in adsorption chemistry, was accomplished using the pH shift method with small modifications as described by [35]. Initially, 50.00 mL of aqueous solutions with 0.100 mol L−1 NaCl were prepared. The solutions' initial pH, ranging from 2.0 to 11.0, was adjusted using 0.100 mol L−1 HCl and NaOH solutions. Subsequently, approximately 50.00 mg of the material was introduced into each solution, followed by mechanical stirring (20 rpm) at room temperature for 24 h. The pHPZC is identified at the pH where the net surface charge of the material becomes neutral, resulting in a zero difference between pHfinal and pHinitial (∆pH). The calculation of ∆pH was performed using the Eq. (1):

The adsorption study of pesticides on Ag@CD@ANS was carried out using the static method in aqueous solution, as described by [36] with minor modifications. For this study, the pesticide atrazine was selected as the standard. In general, about 5 mg of the nanomaterial was added to 1 mL of a solution containing the standard pesticide in different concentrations (ranging between 0.05 and 1.0 mg mL−1) at pH 7.4 ± 0.3. The solutions were maintained under constant agitation for 22 h and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min. After reaching equilibrium, the concentration of pesticide residues was estimated using a Thermo Scientific UV/Vis absorption spectrophotometer and Evolution Array model. The maximum absorption wavelength (230 nm) was selected for the quantification. The amount of pesticide adsorbed at equilibrium (qe, mg g−1) and the extraction efficiency (E) was calculated by the difference between the initial concentration and after adsorption, as described by the equations 2 e 3:

Where, Ci and Ce are the initial concentration and the equilibrium concentration, respectively, (mg mL−1). V is the volume of pesticide standard solution (mL) and M is the mass (mg) of Ag@CD@ANS used.

Mathematical models of adsorption isotherms were also estimated. Adsorption isotherm describes how the adsorbate molecules interact with the surface of the material (adsorbent). Correlation of equilibrium data using theoretical mathematical equations is essential to estimate the type of adsorption that exists. Langmuir isotherm is often applied to explain homogeneous surface adsorption in which all available sites have an identical affinity for the adsorbate. The Freundlich isotherm is based on adsorption involving a heterogeneous (rough) surface of the adsorbent, in which the adsorption sites have different energies of interaction with the adsorbate. The equations that allow calculating the Langmuir (Eq. (4)) and Freundlich (Eq. (5)) isotherms are [21]:

Where qmax is the maximum adsorption capacity (mg g−1). KL is the Langmuir adsorption equilibrium constant (L mg−1) related to the adsorption energy. KF (mg1–1/n L 1/n g−1) is the Freundlich equilibrium constant that indicates the adsorption capacity of the system. n (without dimension) represents the heterogeneity of the system, that is, the adsorption intensity. Generally, n > 1, the isotherm is favorable, and n < 1, the isotherm is unfavorable [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the extraction method using commercial adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: The extraction of the target pesticides (heptachlor, atrazine, DDT, bifenthrin and flutriafol) from shrimp samples was carried out using the MSPD technique. The MSPD extraction technique uses a solid sample homogenized with a solid support and eluted with a specific solvent [37]. The pesticide extraction process by MSPD took place in three stages: (i) homogenization of the material (sample + adsorbent); (ii) preparation of the cartridge containing the mixture and (iii) elution of the analytes of interest (Fig. S2). To obtain the optimal condition for extracting the pesticides, commercial solid supports were tested. For this optimization process, a 23 factorial design was performed. The factors evaluated were: (i) adsorbent/sample ratio (1:1; 1:5; 1:10 m/m); (ii) type of eluent (hexane, Hex; acetonitrile, ACN; and methanol, MeOH); (iii) adsorbent nature (octadecyl, C18; neutral alumina, NA; and silica gel, SG). Table 1 shows the coded levels of the independent variables. Model fitting occurred using central composite analysis (response surface).

Table S2 shows the matrix for the design of experiment (23). The analyzes were performed randomly, ensuring that uncontrollable variables are distributed throughout the experiment, avoiding bias (tendencies). The extractions were performed using 5.00 mL Luer Slip Descarpack cartridges. 10.00 mL of solvents was used to extract the pesticides. The manifold system aided in the extraction, maintaining the flow rate at 0.50 mL min−1. Next, the extract was concentrated by rotary evaporation to a final volume of approximately 1.50 mL. Then, the extract was filtered (0.22 µm) and analyzed by UPLC-DAD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the extraction method using Ag@CD@ANS
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimal condition for extracting the pesticides, using Ag@CD@ANS, a 22 factorial design was performed. The factors evaluated were: (i) sample/adsorbent ratio (1:1; 1:5; 1:10 m/m); (ii) type of eluent (Hex, ACN and MeOH). Table 2 shows the coded levels of the independent variables. Model fitting occurred using central composite analysis (response surface). Table S3 shows the matrix for the design of experiment (22). The analyzes also were performed randomly, avoiding tendencies.

The extractions were performed based on our previous work [8]. Briefly, a portion of the shrimp (0.10 g) was briefly blended with 0.10–1.00 g of solid material (neutral alumina, or Ag@CD@ANS) for 3 min. The homogenized mixture was then transferred to a polyethylene cartridge with a capacity of 5.00 mL, containing silanized glass wool as a support base. Elution was carried out using a vacuum, employing 10.00 mL of organic solvent (Hex, MeOH or ACN). The eluate was collected in a conical tube and concentrated to 1.50 mL. This was achieved first by utilizing a rotary vacuum evaporator at 55 °C, followed by a gentle nitrogen flow. Prior to injection into the LC column, the extracts underwent filtration through a Nylon filter (pore size 0.22 μm, diameter 4 mm; Sartorius, Germany). Then, a 20 μL portion of the extract was directly subjected to analysis by UPLC-DAD.

A Thermo Scientific brand UPLC system implemented with ChromQuest software system, autoinjector (Accela autosampler), pump (Accela 600) and UV–Visible detector (Accela PDA), equipped with a quaternary solvent pump, degasser, and autosampler with an injection volume set to 3 μL. The diode array detector (DAD/UV) was used to separate MSPD shrimp extract residues. Data acquisition and processing were performed using ChromQuest 5.0 software. Chromatographic separation was performed on a reverse-phase Hypersil GOLD analytical column (50 × 2.1 mm internal diameter, particle size 1.9 μm), from Thermo (Torrance, CA, USA). Mobile phases A and B were water and acetonitrile, respectively, delivered at a flow rate of 0.5 mL min−1 at room temperature. After optimization, the isocratic method was used to perform the analysis, keeping the composition constant at 70 % B for 10 min followed by a linear gradient to 100 % B in 5 min and back to the initial conditions in 7 min. Spectral data from all peaks were accumulated in the 190–800 nm range and UV/Vis chromatograms were recorded at 242 nm. Identification of compounds in samples was performed by comparing the characteristics of DAD spectra and the retention time of standard compounds.

Statistical treatment of data and graphs was performed using the STATITISTIC software (version 7). Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) and Tukey's Test were used to determine significant differences between assays at a 95 % confidence level. Triplicate assays were performed.

The functional groups present in the synthesized material were investigated using FTIR. Fig. 2 . A shows the resulting infrared spectra for ANS, Ag@CD and Ag@CD@ANS. From the spectra, it is possible to verify the functionalization of Ag@CD by the ANS. There is a strong absorption band at 3430 cm−1. This absorption peak can be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH. At 2923 cm−1, there is a small absorption peak that corresponds to CH stretching vibration. There is a small absorption peak at 2450 cm−1, which can attribute to CC stretching vibration. Furthermore, there is an obvious main absorption peak at 1593 cm−1 which may be CC stretching vibration. At approximately 1300 cm−1, there is a single absorption peak which may be a SO stretching vibration [38].
The functionalization of the nanomaterial was confirmed by elemental analysis of carbon (C), hydrogen (H) and nitrogen (N). Table 3 shows the percentage of CHN present in the material.

The TEM (Fig. 2B-i and 2B-ii) and SEM (Fig. 2B-iii) images of the synthesized material reveal that Ag@CD@ANS has an irregular surface with a spherical shape. Here, it was considered that the mechanism for the formation of Ag@CD@ANSs is the result of the growth of Ag NPs with the capping agent CDs on their surface (Fig. 2B-ii) [24,39]. In addition, Fig. 2.C shows the XRD pattern of the material, showing the presence of silver (Ag) [40] and a CD pattern that is close to the graphite pattern [41]. By the point of zero-charge experiment (Fig. S3), it was observed that the material has a zero charge point at pH 7.4. At this pH, the global sum of negative and positive charges present on the surface of the nanomaterial is zero. Thus, it was observed that the surface of the material assumes a negative charge for pHs > 7, and a positive charge for pHs < 7, therefore favoring the adsorption of the analyte by adjusting pH.

The thermal stability of the material was evaluated by TGA. From Fig. 2D, it is observed that the Ag@CD@ANS exhibited a first weight loss event in approximately 100 °C, suggesting water loss. After this event, the material showed a loss of mass of 2 %. From then on, Ag@CD@ANS remained stable up to approximately 250 °C. At approximately 480 °C, the material showed a rapid loss of mass of 19 % due to the thermic decomposition. Possibly, in this thermal event, there was a decomposition of the functional groups of ANS. Then, a slow loss of mass of approximately 25 % was observed, suggesting a CD mass loss. After that, the mass of the material remained stable. These results indicate that Ag@CD@ANS has satisfactory thermal stability and that there was satisfactory adhesion of the CD to the silver core during the synthesis process. From the DLS experiments, the AG@CD@ANS particles presented an average size of 132 nm in solution (Fig. 2E-i), showing a size distribution suitable for nanometric systems. It is reasonable to assume that, even with the use of ultrasound before the analyses, the agglomeration state of the material could be present in the system. The zeta potential analysis indicated moderate stability of the prepared material with a value of −32 mV (pH 7.5 ± 0.3) (Fig. 2E-ii).

The adsorption isotherm study (Langmuir and Fruendlich) [21] suggests that the material has a rough (heterogeneous) monolayer surface, corroborating the data obtained by SEM and TEM. Fig. S4 and Table 4 present the curves and the parameters, respectively, of the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms.

The results of this study reveal that the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity (qmax) of the prepared material can be of 208.33 mg of adsorbate per 1.00 g of material, as described by the Langmuir isotherm. In addition, it was observed that the material has a strong interaction with the analyte studied, since it presented “n” equal to 1 and a KF = 66.7 mL1/n mg (1–1/n)/g, as described by the Freundlich isotherm.
To optimize the extraction procedure using MSPD, a multivariate analysis strategy was performed for both adsorbents: (i) commercial adsorbents, and (ii) Ag@CD@ANS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the extraction method using commercial adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: For the commercial adsorbent, a full factorial design (23) was applied, where 2 is the number of levels and 3 is the number of factors tested. This complete factorial design allowed evaluating the independent variables that influenced the extraction. Three factors were evaluated: (i) the nature of the extraction solvent (low, medium, and high polarity), using Hex, ACN and MeOH; (ii) the nature of the adsorbent (low, medium and high polarity), using the commercial adsorbents C18, NA and SG; and (iii) sample:adsorbent ratio (1:1; 1:5 and 1:10). The second phase of the optimization was the application of the response surface methodology, based on a central composite design. This second phase allowed to evaluate the optimal condition of the study, depending on the recovery (%) of pesticides. All statistical data were treated using the STATISTICS 7 software. Fig. S5 shows one of the response surfaces obtained for pesticide recovery, indicating the optimal recovery condition.

From Fig. S5, it is observed that the optimal condition that allowed a greater recovery of the pesticides of interest was using (i) neutral alumina, as adsorbent, (ii) acetonitrile, as eluting solvent, and (ii) the mass ratio 1:3 sample:adsorbent. Table S4 presents the results obtained for the optimization process. Using NA, ACN and sample:adsorbent ratio (1:3) was possible to obtain recovery ranging from 80 to 103 %, with relative standard deviation (RSD) < 2.9% (n = 3) for all the pesticides studied.

The polynomial that represents the quadratic model described in Fig. S5 can be represented by the equation:
Z = 85.9 − 0.6 *
x 1
− 13.5 *
x 2 2
+ 8.6 *
x 1
− 11.9 *
x 3 2
+ 3.5 *
x 2
*
x 3
The goodness of fit of the model was assessed using ANOVA. Table 5 shows the parameters resulting from the data adjustment.

From Table 5, we can see that all independent variables (eluent, adsorbent and mass ratio) show synergism, influencing the extraction of the proposed method. Furthermore, it was observed that for the proposed extraction method, the quadratic model did not show a lack of fit at a 95 % confidence level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the extraction method using Ag@CD@ANS
ce:section.ce:para: For the optimization of the pesticide's extraction using Ag@CD@ANS, a full factorial design (22) was used. Two factors were evaluated: (i) the nature of the extraction solvent (low, medium, and high polarity), using Hex, ACN and MeOH; and (iii) sample:adsorbent ratio (1:1; 1:5 and 1:10). Fig. 3 shows both the response surface (Fig. 3A) and the level curve (Fig. 3B), illustrating the outcomes of pesticide recovery using Ag@CD@ANS. These representations highlight the optimal conditions for achieving maximum recovery. As observed from Fig. 3, the most favorable condition, which facilitated a satisfactory recovery (> 70 %) for the target pesticides, involved the implementation of two factors: (i) employing ACN as the elution solvent, and (ii) using a sample mass ratio of 1:5 with respect to the adsorbent (Ag@CD@ANS). Detailed outcomes of the optimization process are presented in Table S6.

Using the sample:Ag@CD@ANS ratio (m/m) of 1:5, recovery rates ranging from 74 % to 100 % were achievable for all investigated pesticides. Remarkably, RSD remained below 4 % (n = 3), emphasizing the precision of the results.

For Ag@CD@ANS, the polynomial representing the quadratic model described in Fig. 3 can be expressed by the equation:
Z = 56.5 + 29.4 *
x 1
− 13.2 *
x 1 2
+ 15.5 *
x 2
− 3.6 *
x 2 2
+ 0.25 *
x 1
*
x 2
The parameters obtained from the data fitting for the extraction using Ag@CD@ANS are shown in Table 6 .

From the data presented in Table 6, it is evident that the independent variables (eluent, adsorbent/sample and mass ratio) have a significant influence (p < 0.05) on the extraction process using Ag@CD@ANS, while the interaction between the factors does not proved to be significant for the extraction of pesticides using Ag@CD@ANS. Additionally, it was observed that the quadratic model used for the extraction method did not show lack of fit at a 95 % confidence level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the efficiency of the nanomaterial for the extraction of the pesticides
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 presents the chromatographic profiles of all commercial adsorbents used as support for MSPD. From Fig. 4, it is observed that in fact, the NA (Fig. 4.C) simplified the complexity of the matrix (Fig. 4.A) when compared to C18 (Fig. 4.B) and SG (Fig. 4.D), corroborating the data obtained by the optimization (Fig. S5). This fact can be explained by the functional group of the adsorbent. NA [(-Al-OH + -Al-O)] has a greater number of hydroxyl groups on the surface when compared to SG [(SiO2)n-OH)] and especially C18 ([(Si) n -(CH2)17 CH3]), thus increasing the adsorption process between the adsorbent and analyte molecules through hydrogen bonds.

After obtaining the optimal condition, the efficiency of the synthesized material to extract pesticides from shrimp samples was evaluated. Fig. 4.E shows the chromatographic profile of the nanomaterial (Ag@CD@ANS) applied as a support to simplify the complexity of the shrimp sample, using the MSPD technique. As seen in Fig. 5 , like the NA (Fig. 5.C), the synthesized material (Ag@CD@ANS) presented satisfactory efficiency in the shrimp sample treatment process (Fig. 5.D), considerably simplifying the complexity of the sample (Fig. 5.A). Fig. 6 shows the recovery of pesticides after extractions using the synthesized material (Ag@CD@ANS) and the better commercial adsorbent (NA). From Fig. 6, we can see that there is no significant difference (p < 0.05) between the recoveries obtained with NA and Ag@CD@ANS, suggesting that the material can be used as an alternative material to extract the target pesticides.

Regarding the mechanism of adsorption between the analytes and the Ag@CD@ANS material, it is believed that the adsorbent Ag@CD@ANS interacts with the analytes based on predominant, electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, although van der Walls interactions can also exist. The satisfactory extraction efficiency of Ag@CD@ANS can be justified by the functional groups of the ANS coating on the surface of Ag@CD leads to an increase in the interaction of hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl groups of the ANS and the analyte molecules. Furthermore, possible residues of functional groups at CD, that can promote van der Waals interactions, as well as the irregular surface and the presence of porosity can help in the efficiency of the extraction of pesticides, increasing the contact surface and the number of interaction sites. Furthermore, at pH 7.4 the material has a positive charge on its surface, which enables electrostatic interactions with the studied pesticides that are deprotonated (with a negative charge) at pH 7.4. Considering that the pKa of the pesticides vary between 1.7 and 3.5 (Table S1), pH above 3.5 will cause the deprotonation of pesticides, giving them a negative charge.

The proposed method was validated, evaluating the accuracy, precision, selectivity, the limit of detection (LOD), the limit of quantification (LOQ) and robustness. Table 7 presents the calibration data obtained in the method validation process.

Table 7 demonstrates that the proposed method using MSPD has satisfactory sensitivity, with LOQs values varying between 1.6 µg Kg−1 to 3.3 µg Kg−1. The LODs were in the range 0.5–1.0 µg Kg−1. Furthermore, the proposed method showed good linearity for all analytes. The recoveries were calculated based on spiked samples using three fortification levels (10, 25 and 50 µg Kg−1). Table 8 shows the precision and accuracy of the method. The precision values were ≤ 13 %, and the intra-day accuracy was 74–102% (n = 3) (Table 8). The method exhibited good linearity in the range of 5–100 µg Kg−1 with R2 ranging from 0.9983 to 0.9989.

The efficiency and stability of the reuse of Ag@CD@ANS adsorbents to extract the pesticides target was also evaluated. This factor was investigated by studying the recovery of five cycles of adsorption/desorption performed with the same batch of Ag@CD@ANS and ACN under ultrasonic agitation (15 min). After desorption of the analytes, the material was washed with MeOH, dried at 37 °C for 3 h and then reused. From this study, it was observed that there are significant changes in recoveries up to three recycles (Table S7). This indicates that the prepared material is stable and reusable until the third cycle. However, when the fourth run was performed, there was a significant loss of adsorption of the analytes of interest, suggesting a loss of interaction sites.

The proposed method was applied for the determination of organophosphate and organochlorine pesticides in five shrimp samples sold in markets in the city of Viçosa, Minas Gerais, Brazil. As a result, it was observed that the analytical measurements were below the LOD of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison study with previously reported methods
ce:section.ce:para: A comparative study in terms of pesticides extraction efficiency of different adsorbents has been summarized in Table 9 .

The novel synthesized material showed significant performance in extracting POPs in shrimp samples with satisfactory LOD and LOQ, allowing reuse, showing, therefore a potential alternative adsorbent to determine pesticides in food samples.

A novel Ag@CD@ANS nanohybrid material was synthesized and characterized for the extraction of persistent organic pollutants (flutriafol, atrazine, heptachlor, DDT, and bifenthrin) in shrimp samples. The material exhibited a heterogeneous spherical structure with a point of zero charge at pH 7.4. The material showed high adsorption capacity for the target pesticides. Comparable recoveries were obtained between NA and Ag@CD@ANS, with recoveries ranging from 75 % to 105 % (RSD < 15 %, n = 3) and 73 % to 102 % (<13 %, n = 3), respectively. The proposed method achieved satisfactory LOD (≤ 1.0 µg Kg−1) and LOQ (≤ 3.3 µg Kg−1). These findings indicate that Ag@CD@ANS can be a viable support for the extraction of persistent organic pollutants, such as pesticides, in food samples, with acceptable accuracy and precision for multiresidue analysis. In addition, it is important to note that Ag@CD@ANS allows for multiple uses due to its inherent ability to be regenerated after each adsorption cycle. This reuse feature not only contributes to savings, but also aligns with sustainable practices. Therefore, while NA and Ag@CD@ANS exhibit comparable extraction results, the distinct advantage of Ag@CD@ANS lies in its satisfactory recoveries combined with the potential for multiple reuse cycles. This consideration not only addresses effective extraction, but also contributes positively to the overall environmental impact, making Ag@CD@ANS a favorable and sustainable choice for applications that require solids adsorption.
